content.text: To ensure valid, reliable, and fair assessment practices that inform teaching, improve student learning, provide accurate feedback to students and parents, and maintain integrity across both Cambridge and Indonesian assessment requirements.

Eduversal schools implement a balanced assessment philosophy that includes Assessment FOR Learning (formative assessment to support instruction), Assessment AS Learning (student self-reflection and goal setting), and Assessment OF Learning (summative assessment to measure achievement against standards). All assessments are standards-referenced, fair, transparent, and supported by timely, actionable feedback. 1. Assessment FOR Learning (Formative):

items: Assessment used during learning to diagnose needs, adjust instruction, provide feedback
Purpose: Improve learning in real-time, not just measure it
content.title: Core Beliefs

items: Students reflect on own learning, set goals, monitor progress
Purpose: Develop metacognition and self-regulation
Assessment at end of unit/term/course to measure achievement against standards
Purpose: Report proficiency, certify mastery, inform decisions (promotion, graduation)
All assessments aligned to clear learning objectives (Cambridge, Indonesian CP, SLOs)
Students assessed against standards, not against each other (criterion-referenced, not norm-referenced)
content.title: Guiding Principles

items: Assessments accessible to all students (accommodations for EAL, SEN as needed)
Multiple measures used (tests, projects, presentations, portfolios \- not just exams)
Bias minimized (culturally responsive, language-appropriate, diverse examples)
Assessment criteria (rubrics, mark schemes) shared with students before assessment.
Students understand what constitutes quality work.
Grades based on evidence, not effort or behavior (character/effort reported separately)
Feedback provided promptly (within 1-2 weeks for major assessments)
Feedback specific, constructive, and actionable (tells students what to improve and how)
Students given opportunities to apply feedback (revisions, re-assessments when appropriate)
Assessment data used to inform teaching decisions (re-teach, differentiate, accelerate)
Teachers collaborate to analyze data and improve practice (PLCs)

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools employ a comprehensive assessment system balancing multiple types and purposes. Assessment Types:
content.title: Balanced Assessment System

content.text: Purpose: Coordinate assessments across subjects and grades to avoid student overload and ensure adequate time for teaching, learning, and feedback.

items: 2 weeks' notice: Exam schedule published (dates, times, subjects, rooms)
Study guides provided: Teachers distribute review materials, key topics, practice questions
No new content: Week before exams reserved for review, practice, clarification (no introducing new units)
Exam prep sessions: Optional after-school tutoring or review sessions
content.title: Exam Week Protocols

items: Timetable staggered: Maximum 2 exams per day per student (avoid overload)
Breaks between exams: Minimum 1 hour (students can rest, eat, review)
Quiet zones: Library, study areas designated for quiet review during free periods
Invigilation: Exams proctored by teachers (not teaching that subject to avoid bias)
Accommodations: Students with IEPs/access arrangements receive approved support (extra time, separate room, etc.)
Prompt marking: Teachers aim to return marked exams within 1 week
Exam review sessions: Teachers go over exams with students, discuss correct answers, common errors, learning opportunities
Feedback: Written comments on exams indicating strengths and areas for improvement
Annual assessment calendar (published to students/parents via EduNav, website)
Exam schedules (per term)
Study guides and review materials
Exam review session notes

content.text: Purpose: Provide ongoing feedback during learning to help students improve before summative assessments.

items: Process: At end of lesson, students write brief response on index card or digital form
Example: "Write 3 things you learned today, 2 questions you still have, 1 thing you'll practice tonight"
Use: Teacher reviews, identifies misconceptions, adjusts next lesson
Frequency: 2-3 times per week
content.title: High-Impact Formative Assessment Techniques

items: Process: Pose question → students think individually (1 min) → discuss with partner (2 min) → share with class
Use: Checks understanding, encourages participation, identifies gaps in real-time
Frequency: Multiple times per lesson
Process: Teacher asks "How confident are you with this concept?" → Students signal (thumb up \= confident, sideways \= somewhat, down \= need help) or vote via app (Kahoot, Mentimeter, Google Forms)
Use: Quick check of class-wide understanding; differentiate next steps
Frequency: 1-2 times per lesson
Process: Students submit draft of essay/project → receive feedback from teacher or peer using rubric → revise before final submission
Use: Improves quality of final work, teaches students to self-assess and give constructive feedback
Frequency: For major writing assignments, projects
Process: Teacher observes students during independent work, group activities, discussions → records notes on student understanding, skills, behaviors
Use: Informs teaching, identifies students needing support or extension
Frequency: Ongoing; teachers keep notes per student
Process: Students assess own work against rubric or criteria, identify strengths and areas for growth, set goals
Use: Develops metacognition, ownership of learning
Frequency: After major assessments, end of unit
Specific: "Your thesis statement clearly states your argument" (not just "Good job")
Actionable: "To improve, add evidence from the text to support your claim in paragraph 2"
Timely: Provided while student still working on topic or skill (within 1-2 weeks)
Balanced: Acknowledge strengths AND identify areas for growth
Goal-Oriented: Connected to learning objectives and student's individual goals
content.title: Feedback Guidelines

items: Written comments: On student work (margins, end comments)
Rubrics: Checked indicators with brief comments
Verbal feedback: During conferences, in-class check-ins
Audio/Video feedback: Recorded comments (especially useful for detailed feedback on essays or projects)
Peer feedback: Structured peer review using protocols or rubrics

content.text: Purpose: Measure student achievement against standards at end of learning period.

items: Format: Multiple choice, short answer, essay questions
Best for: Assessing knowledge recall, comprehension, analysis
Design principles:
Align questions to learning objectives (test what was taught)
Include range of difficulty (easy to challenging)
Use Bloom's Taxonomy levels (not just recall; include application, analysis, synthesis, evaluation)
Clear instructions and adequate time
content.title: Types of Summative Assessments

items: Format: Students demonstrate skills or create product (lab experiment, oral presentation, debate, drama performance, art portfolio)
Best for: Assessing application, creativity, practical skills, communication
Design principles:
Authentic (real-world relevance)
Clear task description and rubric
Opportunities for planning, practice, feedback before final performance
Format: Extended inquiry or creation over several weeks (research paper, science fair project, documentary film, engineering design challenge)
Best for: Assessing synthesis, creativity, research skills, independence, collaboration (if group project)
Design principles:
Scaffolded checkpoints (proposal, outline, draft, final)
Rubric addressing both process (research, effort, time management) and product (quality, accuracy, presentation)
Balance individual accountability with collaboration (if group)
Format: Curated collection of student work over time showing growth and achievement
Best for: Assessing progress, reflection, range of skills
Design principles:
Clear selection criteria (what to include)
Reflective component (student explains why pieces chosen, what they demonstrate)
Standards-aligned (portfolio mapped to learning objectives)

content.text: Before administering summative assessment, teachers verify: ☐ Alignment: All questions/tasks aligned to taught content and learning objectives ☐ Coverage: Assessment covers breadth of unit (not just one topic) ☐ Validity: Assessment measures what it intends to measure (e.g., math test assesses math, not just reading comprehension of word problems) ☐ Reliability: Assessment would yield consistent results if given again or marked by different teacher (clear rubric, objective criteria) ☐ Fairness: No bias; accessible to all students with appropriate accommodations ☐ Clarity: Instructions clear, questions unambiguous ☐ Time: Adequate time for students to complete (not rushed) ☐ Rubric/Mark Scheme: Prepared in advance, shared with students Evidence:

items: Sample assessments (tests, project descriptions, rubrics) from each subject and grade
Assessment blueprints (table showing learning objectives and corresponding assessment items)
content.title: Assessment Quality Checklist

content.text: Moderation is the process through which teachers collaboratively review student work to ensure that marking is fair, accurate, and consistently aligned to agreed standards across teachers, classes, and time. Moderation protects the integrity of assessment and ensures that students are graded according to the same expectations. Purpose: Ensure consistency and fairness in marking, especially for subjective assessments (essays, projects, performance tasks) and subjects with teacher-assessed components (Cambridge coursework).
Moderation: Process where teachers collaboratively review and discuss student work to ensure their marking aligns with standards and is consistent across classes, teachers, and time.
content.title: What is Moderation?

content.text: 1. Pre-Assessment Standardization (Before Marking): When: Before teachers begin marking major assessment (e.g., end-of-term essays, IGCSE coursework) Process:

items: Select anchor samples: Choose 3-5 student work samples representing range of quality (high, medium, low)
Independent marking: Each teacher marks anchor samples independently using rubric
Compare marks: Teachers share their marks and reasoning
Discuss discrepancies: If marks differ significantly (\>10%), discuss interpretation of rubric, calibrate understanding
Reach consensus: Agree on marks for anchor samples; these become benchmark
content.title: Moderation Process (Step-by-Step)

content.text: Outcome: Teachers aligned on standards before marking full class set 2. During-Marking Moderation (Spot Checks): When: While marking is underway (halfway through marking class set) Process:

items: Sample exchange: Teachers exchange 5-10 scripts with colleague teaching same subject/grade
Blind re-mark: Colleague re-marks without seeing original marks
Compare: If marks within 5-10% → consistency confirmed; if discrepancy \> 10% → discuss and adjust
Adjust if needed: If systemic issue found (e.g., teacher consistently too harsh or lenient), adjust marks for all students

content.text: 3. Post-Marking Moderation (Final Review): When: After all marking completed, before grades finalized Process:

items: Data review: HOD or Academic Coordinator reviews grade distribution (how many A, B, C, etc.)
Check for anomalies: One class has all A's and another all C's for same assessment → investigate
Sample review: HOD reviews sample of marked work from each teacher
Feedback to teachers: If concerns (inconsistent marking, overly harsh/lenient, rubric misapplied), provide feedback and request adjustments

content.text: Outcome: Confidence that grades are fair, accurate, consistent

Requirement: For Cambridge subjects with teacher-assessed coursework (e.g., Art & Design portfolio, Science practical skills), schools must conduct internal moderation before submitting marks to Cambridge (Section 3.13). Process:

steps: Teacher assesses student work using Cambridge mark schemes
Internal moderation: HOD or experienced colleague reviews sample (highest, middle, lowest achieving students)
Adjust marks if needed: Ensure alignment with Cambridge standards
Submit marks to Cambridge: Via Cambridge International Direct
External moderation by Cambridge: Cambridge selects sample to review; may adjust school's marks up or down for all students if discrepancy found
content.title: Moderation for Cambridge Coursework

items: Internal moderation meeting notes (dates, participants, samples reviewed, decisions)
Moderation cover sheets (record of marks before and after moderation)
Anchor samples with marks and annotations
External moderation feedback from Cambridge (if applicable)

content.text: Academic grades reflect student achievement against curriculum standards. Character and effort indicators (such as participation, organization, homework completion, and perseverance) are reported separately and are not included in the calculation of academic grades. Challenge: Eduversal schools report grades for both Cambridge curriculum (international standards) and Indonesian national subjects (Indonesian standards), using different scales and philosophies.

items: Official Cambridge grades: A, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, U (or 9-1 scale for some subjects)
Assigned by Cambridge based on external exam performance (and coursework if applicable)
School-based grades (during course, before external exams):
Schools use predicted grades (what teacher expects student will achieve on final Cambridge exam)
OR percentage/letter grades aligned to Cambridge standards (e.g., 90-100% \= A, 80-89% \= A, etc.)
content.title: Cambridge Subjects: Grading Scale

items: Schools design own grading scale aligned to Cambridge learning objectives
Common scales:
Percentage (0-100%): Clear, quantitative
Letter grades (A-F): A \= Excellent, B \= Good, C \= Satisfactory, D \= Needs Improvement, F \= Fail
Descriptors (1-4 or 1-5 scale):
4 \= Exceeds expectations
3 \= Meets expectations
2 \= Approaching expectations
1 \= Below expectations
Use percentage scale (0-100%) for consistency across schools
Conversion to descriptive grades:
Scale: 0-100 (percentage)
Minimum Passing Grade (KKM \- Kriteria Ketuntasan Minimal): Set by school for each subject, typically 70-75
Descriptors:
90-100 \= Sangat Baik (Excellent)
80-89 \= Baik (Good)
70-79 \= Cukup (Satisfactory)
Below 70 \= Kurang (Needs Improvement; may require remediation)
content.title: Indonesian National Subjects: Grading Scale

items: Separate from subject grades
Scale:
MB (Mulai Berkembang) \- Beginning to Develop
BSH (Berkembang Sesuai Harapan) \- Developing as Expected
SAB (Sudah Berkembang) \- Well Developed
SB (Sangat Berkembang) \- Highly Developed
Each of 6 Profil Pelajar Pancasila dimensions rated separately (Beriman, Berkebinekaan Global, Bergotong Royong, Mandiri, Bernalar Kritis, Kreatif)

content.text: Philosophy: Academic grades reflect achievement against standards; character/effort grades reflect habits, behaviors, attitudes. Character/Effort Indicators:

items: Participation: Engages in class discussions, asks questions, contributes to group work
Homework Completion: Submits assignments on time, demonstrates effort
Organization: Comes prepared with materials, keeps work neat and organized
Perseverance: Persists when work is challenging, seeks help when needed
Respect: Treats others respectfully, follows classroom norms
content.title: Character/Effort Grades (Separate from Academic Grades)

items: E (Excellent): Consistently demonstrates positive habits
S (Satisfactory): Usually demonstrates positive habits
N (Needs Improvement): Inconsistently demonstrates positive habits; improvement plan needed
Character/effort grades appear in separate section of report card (not averaged into academic GPA)

content.text: Purpose: Communicate student progress to students and parents clearly, accurately, and in ways that support continued learning. EduNav is the primary platform for recording assessment data, reporting grades, and communicating student progress to parents and students. Assessment information entered by teachers must align with the guidelines and weighting structures defined in this Assessment Policy.

items: Mid-Term Progress Reports (Informal): Week 8-10 of each term (brief update, no formal grades yet)
Format: Email or EduNav message from homeroom teacher or subject teachers
Content: "On track," "excelling," or "concern" for each subject; brief comment
End-of-Term Report Cards (Formal): Week 20 (Term 1) and Week 24 (Term 2)
Format: Multi-page printed or digital report card
Content: Detailed grades, teacher comments, attendance, character/effort
content.title: Report Card Schedule

items: Student name, grade, homeroom teacher
School name, logo, academic year
Reporting period (Term 1 or Term 2)
content.title: Report Card Components

items: Subjects listed (English, Math, Sciences, Humanities, Electives)
Grades (percentage or letter grade; predicted Cambridge grade if applicable)
Teacher comments (2-3 sentences per subject):
Strengths: What student does well
Areas for growth: What to work on
Recommendation: How to improve (specific actions)

content.text: Example Comment: "Sarah demonstrates excellent analytical skills in Biology, particularly in her lab reports. To further strengthen her understanding, she should focus on linking theoretical concepts to real-world applications. I recommend reviewing past exam questions to prepare for the IGCSE external exam." 3. Indonesian National Subjects Section:

items: Subjects listed (Bahasa Indonesia, PPKn, Religion, Mulok)
Grades (0-100 scale)
Teacher comments (in Bahasa Indonesia)
Six dimensions (Beriman, Berkebinekaan Global, Bergotong Royong, Mandiri, Bernalar Kritis, Kreatif)
Rating for each (MB, BSH, SAB, SB)
Overall Profil Pelajar Pancasila comment (how student demonstrates Profil Pelajar Pancasila values)
Indicators (Participation, Homework, Organization, Perseverance, Respect)
Rating (E, S, N)
Days Present / Total Days
Absences: Excused, unexcused, late arrivals
Comment if attendance concern
Activities (clubs, sports, service)
Brief note on contribution
Holistic summary (academic progress, social-emotional development, character growth)
Goals for next term
Parent partnership: How parents can support at home
Homeroom teacher, Principal/GM, Parent/Guardian (parent signs to acknowledge receipt)
IEP students' reports include progress toward IEP goals (separate section or addendum)
Grades may reflect modified standards (if applicable), clearly indicated
Comments reference accommodations and support provided
content.title: Reporting for Students with IEPs

content.text: Sample Report Card Excerpt (Grade 10 IGCSE Student)
Purpose: Complement written reports with face-to-face dialogue, answer parent questions, co-create support plans.

items: Term 1: Week 20 (after Term 1 report cards distributed)
Mid-Year (Optional/As Needed): Week 10 of Term 2 (for students with concerns)
Term 2: Week 24 (after final report cards; often combined with graduation/end-of-year celebration)
content.title: Conference Schedule

items: 10-15 minute appointments per family (longer for students with IEPs or significant concerns)
Student-led conferences (optional): Student presents own work and progress to parents with teacher facilitating (especially effective Grades 7+)
Teacher greets family warmly, thanks them for attending
Brief positive opening ("It's been a pleasure having Andi in class this term")
content.title: Conference Structure

items: Share report card, highlight strengths
Discuss areas for growth with specific examples
Show student work samples (tests, projects, writing)
Answer parent questions about grades, assessments
Discuss student's behavior, participation, friendships, wellbeing
Note Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies and character growth

content.text: 4. Goal Setting and Action Plan (2-3 min):

items: Collaboratively set 1-2 goals for next term (academic and/or behavioral)
Identify specific actions:
Student will: (e.g., complete homework daily, ask questions when confused, read 20 minutes/night)
Teacher will: (e.g., provide extra practice materials, check in weekly, offer tutoring)
Parent will: (e.g., monitor homework completion, limit screen time, ensure adequate sleep)
Summarize agreements
Thank family for partnership
Provide contact information for follow-up
Review student data (grades, assessments, attendance, behavior)
Prepare talking points (2-3 strengths, 1-2 areas for growth)
Gather work samples
Anticipate parent questions/concerns
content.title: Preparation for Conferences

items: Review report card before conference
Prepare questions
Bring concerns or observations from home
Conference schedules and appointment confirmations
Conference notes/summary forms (teacher records key points, goals, action plan)
Follow-up communication logs (emails checking on progress)

subsections.title: Use of Assessment Data to Inform Instruction

content.text: Purpose: Assessment is not endpoint; it's a tool to improve teaching and learning.

What are PLCs? Professional Learning Communities: Teams of teachers (same subject, same grade, or cross-grade) meeting regularly to analyze student data, share strategies, and improve practice. PLC Meeting Cycle (Every 2-3 weeks): 1. Review Data (15 min):

items: Teachers bring assessment data (recent quiz, unit test, formative assessments)
Identify patterns:
Strengths: Which learning objectives did most students master?
Gaps: Which objectives did many students struggle with?
Outliers: Any students significantly above or below class average?
content.title: Data Analysis Process (Professional Learning Communities)

items: Why did students struggle?
Insufficient instructional time?
Concept too abstract?
Language barriers (EAL)?
Prerequisite skills missing?
Assessment unclear or too difficult?
For whole class: Re-teach concept using different approach (more visuals, hands-on activity, real-world example)
For small groups: Targeted intervention during class (pull small group for reteaching while others work independently) or after school
For individuals: One-on-one tutoring, modified assignment, referral to EAL/SEN support
Teachers share what worked well (successful lesson, effective assessment, differentiation strategy)
Borrow ideas from colleagues

content.text: 5. Set Goals for Next Meeting (5 min):

items: Commit to trying new strategy
Plan next assessment to monitor progress
Schedule follow-up
PLC meeting agendas and notes
Data analysis protocols (templates for reviewing data)
Intervention plans
Evidence of impact (before-and-after assessment data showing improvement after intervention)

content.text: 6. Teacher Professional Development Based on Data: DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency): Network-wide professional development program addressing gaps identified through assessment data, Appraisal results, and PLC discussions
Example 1: Grade 8 Math (Algebra Unit) Root Cause: Instruction moved too quickly; students needed more practice with scaffolded problems before tackling complex multi-step equations Intervention:

items: Whole Class: Reteach multi-step equations with explicit step-by-step modeling; use color-coding to show each step
Small Group: Pull 10 students for additional practice during next week's independent work time
Individual: 3 students referred to after-school math support for foundational gaps in operations
content.title: Data-Driven Decision Making Examples

content.text: Re-Assessment: Quiz given 1 week after intervention Result: 85% of students now score above 70%; significant improvement Next Steps: Continue scaffolding; build in more frequent practice opportunities; monitor progress closely Example 2: Grade 10 IGCSE English (Essay Writing) Data: EASE Cycle 2 writing scores show 40% of Grade 10 students below proficiency in argumentative writing Analysis: Students struggle with:

items: Organizing arguments logically
Providing textual evidence
Analyzing evidence (not just quoting)
Students have limited experience with the argumentative genre
Need explicit instruction in structure and analytical writing
Curriculum Adjustment: Add 2-week unit on argumentative writing before next essay assignment
Model essays (analyze structure, thesis statements, evidence use)
Practice writing thesis statements and topic sentences
Teach "claim-evidence-analysis" paragraph structure
Scaffolding: Provide graphic organizers (essay outline template)
Peer Review: Students exchange drafts, give feedback using rubric before final submission
EAL Support: Push-in support during writing lessons for EAL students; pre-teach academic vocabulary

content.text: Re-Assessment: Next essay assignment (1 month later) Result: 70% now proficient or above; ongoing support for remaining students
Purpose: Cultivate culture of honesty, original thinking, and proper attribution; prevent and address academic dishonesty.

items: Plagiarism: Using someone else's words, ideas, or work without proper citation (includes copying from internet, books, peers)
Cheating: Using unauthorized materials or assistance during assessments (cheat sheets, copying answers, using phone)
Collusion: Unauthorized collaboration (working together on individual assignment and submitting similar work)
Fabrication: Making up data, sources, or citations
Facilitation: Helping another student cheat (sharing answers, letting someone copy)
AI Misuse: Submitting AI-generated work without attribution or using AI inappropriately (e.g., AI writes entire essay when task requires original student writing)
content.title: Academic Integrity Policy

items: Primary (Grades 1-6): Age-appropriate lessons on:
What is copying vs. own work
How to paraphrase (put in own words)
When to give credit (if you learned from someone/something, say where)
Secondary (Grades 7-12): Formal instruction on:
Citation styles (MLA, APA, Chicago \- depending on subject)
Paraphrasing vs. quoting vs. summarizing
How to integrate sources into writing
When citation required (direct quotes, paraphrases, statistics, ideas)
content.title: Prevention Strategies

items: Process-based assignments: Require drafts, outlines, research logs (harder to plagiarize if process documented)
Unique prompts: Change essay questions/project topics each year (can't copy from previous students)
In-class components: Include in-class writing, presentations, or checkpoints (verifies student understands and can do work independently)
Authentic tasks: Real-world problems or creative prompts less likely to have ready-made answers online
Plagiarism detection software: Turnitin, Grammarly Plagiarism Checker, or similar (students submit essays electronically; software checks against a database)
Educational use: Show students similarity report, discuss proper citation (not just punitive)
AI detection tools: GPTZero, Turnitin AI detector (use judiciously; not 100% accurate; combine with other evidence)
Value learning over grades: Emphasize growth, effort, improvement (reduces pressure to cheat)
Normalize asking for help: Make it clear that seeking teacher/tutor assistance is encouraged (not cheating)
Honor code or integrity pledge: Students sign a commitment to honesty at start of year

content.text: If Plagiarism/Cheating Suspected: Step 1: Gather Evidence

items: Teacher documents concern (suspicious similarity to online source, unusual change in writing quality, identical answers to peer, etc.)
Run plagiarism check or compare work samples
content.title: Detection and Response

items: Teacher meets with student privately, presents evidence
Ask student to explain (give benefit of doubt; may be misunderstanding rather than intentional dishonesty)
Student may admit or deny

content.text: Step 3: Determine Violation Level and Consequence Minor Violation (First offense, unintentional, limited scope):

items: Example: Student forgot to cite one source in bibliography; paraphrased without citation but didn't copy verbatim
Consequence:
Educational conversation (explain why it's wrong, how to avoid)
Redo assignment with proper citations
Warning recorded but no grade penalty (or minor penalty, e.g., \-10%)
Example: Copied several paragraphs from website without citation; collaborated inappropriately on individual assignment
Consequence:
Zero on assignment or reduced grade (e.g., 50% maximum)
Parent notification
Reflection essay or academic integrity workshop
Recorded in student file

content.text: Major Violation (Serious, repeated, or high-stakes assessment):

items: Example: Submitted entirely plagiarized essay; used phone to cheat on exam; facilitated cheating for others
Consequence:
Zero on assignment/exam
Referral to Principal/GM (disciplinary action may include suspension)
Parent meeting
May impact grade for course or eligibility for honors
Recorded in student file; may be noted on transcript for university applications (depending on severity)
Teacher completes Academic Integrity Incident Report
Includes: Description of violation, evidence, student response, consequence, follow-up plan
Stored in student file and discipline records

content.text: For Cambridge exams or coursework: Follow Section 3.10 procedures (must report to Cambridge; Cambridge investigates and imposes sanctions)
content.title: Cambridge-Specific Malpractice

content.text: AI tools may be used to support learning for tasks such as idea generation, research support, language development, and proofreading. However, students must always submit work that reflects their own understanding and original thinking. Using AI to generate full answers, essays, or solutions that are then submitted as the student’s own work constitutes academic misconduct. Detailed guidelines for acceptable AI use are described in the Academic Standards — Section 6.12. Permitted AI Use:

items: Brainstorming: Generate topic ideas, outline structures
Grammar/spelling check: Grammarly, spell-checkers for proofreading
Translation support: Translate key terms or phrases for comprehension (EAL students)
Research assistant: Ask AI to explain difficult concepts, suggest resources (but verify accuracy)
content.title: AI and Academic Integrity Guidelines

items: Writing essays or responses: AI writes content that the student submits as their own work
Solving problems: AI solves math problems, science questions, etc., and student copies without understanding
Creating projects/presentations: AI generates slides, posters, artwork submitted as the student's original creation

content.text: Guideline: If the assignment assesses students' writing, thinking, or problem-solving, AI should not do that work. AI can support process but not replace student's intellectual effort. Declaration: For major assignments, students sign statement: "I certify this is my own work. I used [tools/resources] for [purpose]. All AI-generated content is clearly marked and cited." Evidence:

items: Academic integrity policy (published in student handbook, website)
Signed integrity pledges (start of year)
Lesson plans on citation and integrity
Plagiarism check reports (Turnitin summaries)
Academic integrity incident reports (anonymized summaries for review; individual reports confidential)

content.text: Principle: Fair assessment means giving every student opportunity to demonstrate what they know and can do, which sometimes requires different conditions or formats.
For Students with IEPs or Documented Needs: Time:

items: Extended time (typically 25% extra, e.g., 90 min test → 112 min)
Frequent breaks (5-10 min break every 30-45 min)
content.title: Accommodations (Do Not Change Standards)

items: Separate quiet room (minimize distractions)
Small group setting (less anxiety)
Preferential seating (front of room, away from window)
Enlarged print (font size 14-18)
Read-aloud (teacher or software reads questions to student)
Simplified language (reword complex instructions without changing content)
Visual aids (graphic organizers, diagrams)
Scribe (student dictates answers; scribe writes)
Word processor (typing instead of handwriting)
Oral response (student answers verbally instead of writing)
Dictation software (speech-to-text)

steps: Identification: Student assessed by psychologist/specialist; need documented in IEP
Approval: Accommodations approved by Academic Coordinator/SEN Coordinator
Implementation: Teachers implement accommodations for all assessments (classroom tests and Cambridge exams if approved by Cambridge)
Documentation: Accommodations noted in student file; evidence of use maintained

content.text: For All Students (Not Just SEN): Choice Boards:

items: Students choose how to demonstrate learning from menu of options
Example (Grade 9 Science \- Cell Unit):
Option A: Written test (multiple choice \+ short answer)
Option B: Create 3D cell model with labeled parts \+ oral explanation
Option C: Produce video explaining cell structure and function
All options assess same learning objectives but cater to different strengths
content.title: Differentiated Assessment (Adjust to Readiness/Interest)

items: Same learning objective, different complexity levels
Example (Grade 8 Math \- Fractions):
Tier 1 (Approaching): Add/subtract fractions with like denominators
Tier 2 (Meeting): Add/subtract fractions with unlike denominators
Tier 3 (Exceeding): Solve word problems involving multiple operations with fractions
Allow students to choose format (essay, presentation, poster, video, podcast, etc.) as long as content requirements met
IEP/accommodation documentation
Differentiated assessment examples (choice boards, tiered tasks)
Student work samples showing varied approaches

content.text: Purpose: Compare student performance to external standards, track growth over time, inform school improvement.
What: Eduversal network-wide standardized assessment administered 4x/year Who: All students Grades 1-12 (except Grade 11, 13 during Cambridge external exam periods) What It Measures:

items: Literacy: Reading comprehension, writing
Numeracy: Mathematical reasoning, problem-solving
Subject-Specific Content: Science, social studies (varies by grade)
content.title: EASE (Evaluation of Achievement Standardized Exam)

items: Online platform (Edutama/EASE system)
Scheduled windows: Week 10 (Cycle 1), Week 4 (Cycle 2), Week 11 (Cycle 3), Week 18 (Cycle 4) per calendar
Proctored by teachers (not high-stakes; classroom-based)
Individual Student Reports: Show performance by skill/standard; growth from previous cycle
Class Reports: Aggregate data for teacher (which objectives mastered, which need reteaching)
School Reports: Overall school performance; comparison to Eduversal network average
Eduversal Network Reports: Benchmarking across all 15+ partner schools
Teachers adjust instruction based on results
Schools identify professional development needs
Eduversal monitors network-wide quality
NOT used for individual student grades (formative/diagnostic purpose)
EASE administration schedules
Individual and aggregate EASE reports
PLC meeting notes analyzing EASE data and planning interventions
Quick results: Latihan.id dashboard (immediately after test)
Comprehensive reports: Edunav \> EASE \> EASE Keys and Results

steps: Individual Student Reports: Question-by-question breakdown, strengths/weaknesses, comparison to class/school/national average
Class Reports: High/low achievers, topic-by-topic evaluation, class strengths
Validity Reports: Question quality analysis for continuous improvement
Topic Evaluation Reports: Subject-specific performance vs network schools
Certificates of Achievement: Top 3 students per subject (motivation/recognition)

items: School Management: Evaluate T\&L effectiveness, identify improvement areas, support accreditation
Teachers: Identify underperformance causes, personalize instruction
Students: Understand strengths/weaknesses, take ownership of learning
Parents: Track progress, support learning at home
EASE reports (all five types, anonymized samples)
PLC meeting notes analyzing EASE data
Intervention plans based on EASE findings

content.text: What: Optional diagnostic tests from Cambridge for Grades 7-9 (Lower Secondary) in English, Math, Science Purpose:

items: Assess readiness for next stage (particularly IGCSE)
Identify strengths and gaps
Benchmark against international standards
content.title: Cambridge Progression Tests

items: Schools may administer annually (typically end of year, Week 16-18)
Online or paper-based
Inform decisions about tier placement (e.g., Extended vs Core Math for IGCSE)
Guide intervention for students below the expected level
NOT high-stakes; not reported on transcript

content.text: What: Indonesian national assessment (Section 2.16) Who: Sample of students in Grades 5, 8, 11 What It Measures:

items: Literacy and Numeracy (AKM \- Asesmen Kompetensi Minimum)
Character (Survei Karakter \- student wellbeing, P8 values)
Learning Environment (Survei Lingkungan Belajar \- teacher and principal complete)
content.title: ANBK (Asesmen Nasional Berbasis Komputer)

items: Centrally scheduled by the Ministry of Education (typically August-September)
Computer-based
School provides devices and proctors
School-level results only (not individual student grades)
Results published in Rapor Pendidikan (national education report card)
Comparison to national, provincial, and similar schools
School improvement planning (identify areas needing support)
National education policy and resource allocation
Results inform quality management discussions with Yayasan and Ministry
ANBK administration documentation
School-level results from Rapor Pendidikan portal (ministry website)
SIP (School Improvement Plan) reflecting ANBK findings

subsections.title: Reporting to Universities and Transcript Management

content.text: Purpose: Provide accurate, comprehensive academic records for university admission, scholarships, and transfers.

items: Student Information: Name, date of birth, student ID
School Information: School name, address, curriculum type (Cambridge \+ Indonesian)
Academic Record:
Grades for all courses taken (Grades 9-12 typically; some universities request earlier)
Course titles, credit hours (if applicable), grades, year taken
Separate sections for Cambridge subjects and Indonesian national subjects
Standardized Test Results:
Cambridge IGCSE, AS, A Level results (official grades from Cambridge)
ANBK participation noted (if applicable)
GPA (Grade Point Average):
Calculated on a 4.0 scale (or other scale specified)
Weighted or unweighted (clearly indicated)
Class Rank (Optional): If the school ranks students
Graduation Date: Expected or actual
School Profile: Summary of school curriculum, grading scale, available courses, university outcomes (helps universities contextualize grades)
content.title: Official Transcripts

items: Official transcripts: Sealed envelope with school stamp, or sent electronically via secure platform (Parchment, SLATE, etc.)
Unofficial transcripts: Student/parent may view via EduNav; not valid for applications
Student submits transcript request form (online via EduNav or paper form)
Specifies: Recipient (university name/address), delivery method (mail, electronic, pickup), number of copies
Processing time: 3-5 business days
Fee may apply (especially for rush requests or international mailing)

content.text: Predicted grades are professional teacher judgments indicating the grade a student is most likely to achieve in their final Cambridge examinations. Predicted grades are confidential and are normally shared only with universities or scholarship bodies as part of official school submissions. They are based on evidence such as class assessments, mock examinations, coursework, and teacher professional judgement. Context: Many universities (especially in the UK, Australia, and Canada) require predicted grades for students applying before the final Cambridge results are available. Process:

steps: Teacher Assessment: Subject teacher predicts the grade the student is likely to achieve on the Cambridge external exam (based on mock exams, coursework, and classwork performance)
Review: Principal/Academic Coordinator reviews predictions for consistency
Letter: School issues official predicted grades letter on school letterhead, signed by Principal
Confidentiality: Predictions shared with universities but typically not with students/parents (to avoid pressure)
content.title: Predicted Grades for University Applications

content.text: Accuracy Imperative: Predictions should be realistic; over-predicting harms students the (university may reject if actual grades are much lower) and damages school credibility.
Purpose: Provide qualitative assessment of student's academic abilities, character, potential (complement grades/test scores). Process:

items: Student requests recommendation from teacher(s) and/or counselor (typically 2-3 letters required by universities)
Student provides: Resume/CV, personal statement draft, university list, deadline
Teacher writes letter addressing:
How long/in what capacity they know student
Academic strengths and specific examples
Character qualities (curiosity, resilience, leadership, integrity)
Comparison to peers (if appropriate)
Endorsement and confidence in student's success at university
Teacher submits directly to university (confidential; student typically waives right to read)
content.title: Letters of Recommendation

items: Students request letters at least 4-6 weeks before deadline (earlier for popular teachers)
Transcript request logs
Predicted grade documentation
Recommendation letter request forms (record of who requested, from whom, deadline)
University outcomes data (where students admitted; correlation with predicted vs actual grades for continuous improvement)

subsections.title: Assessment Policy Review and Continuous Improvement

content.text: Purpose: Assessment practices evolve based on research, student needs, stakeholder feedback.
When: End of academic year (May-June) Who: Assessment Committee (Academic Coordinator, HODs, selected teachers, SEN Coordinator, Principal/GM) Process: 1. Data Review:

items: Student achievement trends (are students improving year-over-year?)
Assessment reliability and validity (are assessments measuring what they should? Are results consistent?)
Moderation effectiveness (are marks fair and consistent?)
Use of accommodations (are students receiving needed support?)
content.title: Annual Assessment Policy Review

items: Teacher survey (workload, clarity of grading policies, moderation usefulness)
Student survey (fairness of assessments, clarity of expectations, usefulness of feedback)
Parent survey (communication of grades, understanding of reports)
Review educational research on assessment (new strategies, tools)
Benchmark against other schools (Eduversal network, international schools)
Update grading scales, rubrics, reporting formats if needed
Add new assessment types (e.g., incorporate more performance tasks)
Improve turnaround time for feedback
Strengthen moderation processes
Update Assessment Policy document
Communicate changes to staff, students, parents
Plan PD sessions for teachers on assessment practices (designing quality assessments, effective feedback, using data)
CIS/WASC accreditation self-study (Section 18) \- assessment is major focus area
Eduversal Supervisi visits (Section 2.21) \- quality assurance includes assessment review
content.title: Accreditation Alignment

items: Assessment policy (current version with revision history)
Assessment committee meeting notes
Survey results (teachers, students, parents on assessment)
Professional development on assessment (attendance records, session materials)
Examples of assessment improvements implemented based on review

content.text: The Appraisal Program evaluates teaching quality through:

steps: Subject Appraisals: 35-45 min classroom observations across core subjects
School Appraisal: Evaluation across five domains (School Development, Management & Leadership, Subject Departments, Olympiad & Educational Technology, Curriculum/Teaching/Assessment)
Conducted by the Eduversal Appraisal Team annually
Results inform professional development planning (DTP)
Appraisal observation forms and rubrics
Post-appraisal meeting notes
Teacher improvement plans
Cross-reference: See Section 9.X for full Appraisal Program details

content.text: Comprehensive Assessment Documentation: All assessment data, records, and reports are handled confidentially in accordance with Indonesian data protection regulations and Eduversal policy. Record-keeping requirements, storage procedures, and retention periods are defined in the Assessment Policy. Policy and Procedures:

items: Assessment Policy (formative, summative, moderation, grading, reporting, integrity)
Academic Integrity Policy
Accommodations and Differentiation Guidelines
AI Usage Guidelines
Sample assessments (tests, rubrics, project descriptions) from each subject/grade
Assessment blueprints (alignment to standards)
Moderation cover sheets and protocols
Annual assessment calendar (all grades)
Exam schedules (PTS, PAS, Cambridge mocks, external exams)
EASE reports (individual, class, school, network)
Cambridge results (Checkpoint, IGCSE, AS/A Level) \- past 3 years
ANBK results (school-level)
PLC meeting notes with data analysis
Report card templates (Cambridge subjects, Indonesian subjects, Profil Pelajar Pancasila, character)
Sample report cards (redacted for privacy)
Parent-Teacher Conference schedules and notes
Transcripts (official and unofficial formats)
Academic Integrity Incident Reports (anonymized summaries)
Plagiarism detection reports (Turnitin summaries)
Student integrity pledges
IEP/accommodation documentation
Access arrangement approvals (Cambridge)
Implementation logs
Assessment-focused PD session materials
Teacher training on rubrics, moderation, feedback
Cambridge Assessment Standards: Internal and external moderation procedures; coursework integrity; secure exam administration; fair and valid assessment
Indonesian Curriculum Standards: Assessment aligned to Capaian Pembelajaran (CP); Profil Pelajar Pancasila competency assessment; reporting requirements
CIS Standards: Teaching & Assessing for Learning (assessment drives instruction, valid and reliable, fair and ethical, communicated clearly)
WASC Category B: Curriculum, Instruction, and Assessment (Criterion B3 \- assessment practices valid, reliable, equitable; results used to improve learning)
Eduversal SLOs: Assessment measures progress toward SLOs; SLO-linked reporting to parents
